selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
No empty fields nor ... are allowed. Do not skip any field.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":82,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["painting","cinema","football (calcio)","travel"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":59,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":26,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":66,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":1,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

persona = {"age":82,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["painting","cinema","football (calcio)","travel"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":59,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":26,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":66,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":1,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":82,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"no formal education","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"lower secondary"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":1,"migration_status":"none","...} }{" :"}{" } ,"opinion":"I have spent most of my life in Kansas City, watching the world change from horse-drawn carriages to electric cars, and now I see a new kind of speed—information that travels faster than any train or plane. Even though I do not use social media myself, I hear stories from friends who are younger, and I feel compelled to share what I have learned about keeping clear of the falsehoods that can spread like wildfire on those platforms. The first lesson is simple: treat every headline as a question rather than an answer. When you see something that sounds sensational—perhaps a claim that a new miracle cure exists or that a celebrity has made a shocking announcement—pause and ask yourself whether it comes from a source you trust. In my days, I would have asked the newspaper editor; today, I recommend checking if the article is posted by an official account or a verified news outlet. If not, look for corroboration: does another reputable site report the same story? If the answer is no, that is a red flag. The second step is to use tools that help you sift through noise. Many browsers now offer extensions that flag potential misinformation; some social media platforms themselves have fact‑checking labels. I suggest installing one of these before you even open your phone or tablet. Thirdly, remember the value of context. A single sentence taken out of a longer article can be misleading. If you come across a quote that seems outrageous, search for the full interview or original source. Often, the truth is buried in the details that are omitted when someone tries to make a point quickly. Fourth, cultivate a habit of cross‑checking with reliable databases. For health claims, consult sites like Mayo Clinic or WebMD; for scientific studies, look at PubMed or Google Scholar. Even if you do not read every paper, knowing where trustworthy information resides can help you decide whether to believe something that sounds too good to be true. Finally, share responsibly. If you feel compelled to forward a piece of news, add your own note: “I’m sharing this because I found it interesting, but please verify.” By doing so, you encourage others to think critically rather than simply pass along rumors. In the end, managing fake content is less about technology and more about habits—questioning, verifying, and communicating with care. These practices will keep your mind clear of misinformation, just as a well‑painted fence keeps weeds from creeping into a garden.","reasoning_summary":"1. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: I have spent most of my life in Kansas City, watching the world change from horse-drawn carriages to electric cars, and now I see a new kind of speed—information that travels faster than any train or plane. Even though I do not use social media myself, I hear stories from friends who are younger, and I feel compelled to share what I have learned about keeping clear of the falsehoods that can spread like wildfire on those platforms. The first lesson is simple: treat every headline as a question rather than an answer. When you see something that sounds sensational—perhaps a claim that a new miracle cure exists or that a celebrity has made a shocking announcement—pause and ask yourself whether it comes from a source you trust. In my days, I would have asked the newspaper editor; today, I recommend checking if the article is posted by an official account or a verified news outlet. If not, look for corroboration: does another reputable site report the same story? If the answer is no, that is a red flag. The second step is to use tools that help you sift through noise. Many browsers now offer extensions that flag potential misinformation; some social media platforms themselves have fact‑checking labels. I suggest installing one of these before you even open your phone or tablet. Thirdly, remember the value of context. A single sentence taken out of a longer article can be misleading. If you come across a quote that seems outrageous, search for the full interview or original source. Often, the truth is buried in the details that are omitted when someone tries to make a point quickly. Fourth, cultivate a habit of cross‑checking with reliable databases. For health claims, consult sites like Mayo Clinic or WebMD; for scientific studies, look at PubMed or Google Scholar. Even if you do not read every paper, knowing where trustworthy information resides can help you decide whether to believe something that sounds too good to be true. Finally, share responsibly. If you feel compelled to forward a piece of news, add your own note: “I’m sharing this because I found it interesting, but please verify.” By doing so, you encourage others to think critically rather than simply pass along rumors. In the end, managing fake content is less about technology and more about habits—questioning, verifying, and communicating with care. These practices will keep your mind clear of misinformation, just as a well‑painted fence keeps weeds from creeping into a garden.
